01 · About

A little about me

I'm Govind Reddy Rajeshwari, a 3rd-year B.E. student specialising in Artificial Intelligence & Data Science at Nalla Malla Reddy Engineering College, Hyderabad.

My focus lies at the intersection of AI systems, data analytics, and intelligent application development. I enjoy building end-to-end solutions — from training ML models and crafting Streamlit dashboards to architecting multi-agent AI pipelines.

I complement my academic work with real-world simulations (Deloitte, Tata) and industry certifications (Google Analytics, IBM) to ensure my skills stay grounded in practical application.

PROJECT 01 · 2026

Sentinel MAS

PROJECT 01 · 2026

Sentinel MAS

Live Demo → GitHub
StackPythonStreamlitGroq LLMllama-3.3-70bSQLAlchemyAPSchedulerJWT

Implements the Gosmar & Dahl (2025) Coordinator-Sentinel pattern. A central Coordinator Agent orchestrates 5 specialised Sentinel Agents, each owning a pipeline stage. All agents share state through a SQLAlchemy-backed store with APScheduler driving continuous monitoring.

COORDINATOR AGENT (Groq llama-3.3-70b + HITL Panel) | |——————|——————|——————|——————————————————| | | | | | Discovery Scanning Analysis Reporting Monitoring + Fraud Detection Agent Agent Agent Agent (APScheduler — continuous) | Shared State (SQLAlchemy + JWT Auth + TIVS Score Log)

Sentinel MAS operates on live system inputs. Below are the primary data sources ingested during a vulnerability assessment session.

Data SourceTypeUsed ByDescription
System Network ScanLive / Real-timeDiscovery AgentOpen ports, active hosts, network topology discovered during assessment
CVE Database (NVD)External APIScanning AgentNIST National Vulnerability Database matched against discovered services
TIVS Score LogGenerated / SQLiteMonitoring AgentTime-series threat and vulnerability scores persisted via SQLAlchemy
User Session LogsJWT / SessionFraud DetectionAuthenticated session data monitored for anomalous access patterns
Agent Decision LogsInternal / SQLiteAll AgentsFull audit trail of all agent decisions and coordinator instructions
6
AI Agents
<2s
Agent Response
70B
LLM Parameters
100%
HITL Coverage
Real-time
TIVS Monitoring

Groq's inference infrastructure enables sub-2-second agent response times using llama-3.3-70b. The Coordinator successfully orchestrates all 6 agents with zero message-passing failures in testing. HITL provides supervisory override at every decision checkpoint, ensuring human control is never fully relinquished.

Architecture
Implementing the Coordinator-Sentinel Pattern: A First Open-Source Attempt
The Gosmar & Dahl (2025) paper introduced this architecture for autonomous multi-agent security systems. This post walks through translating the theory into a working Python implementation — agent communication, SQLAlchemy state management, and why Groq was chosen for production speed.
Engineering
Real-Time Threat Scoring: Building the TIVS Engine
TIVS is the heartbeat of Sentinel MAS. This post explains how the scoring formula was designed, how APScheduler drives continuous re-evaluation, and how scores are persisted and visualised — giving teams an always-current risk posture view.
Security Philosophy
Human-in-the-Loop AI: Why Full Automation Isn't the Answer
Fully autonomous security systems carry significant risk. This post makes the case for HITL design — what decisions are always escalated to the human operator and lessons learned about trust calibration in agentic AI systems.

Real screenshots from the live Sentinel MAS deployment. All 6 views shown below.

Guardian Control Center: 6 agents idle, TIVS 1.0/10 SECURE, live pipeline status
New Scan: 6-agent pipeline running sequentially with timestamped progress log
Scan complete: Grade D · Risk 7.5/10 · 3 confirmed vulnerabilities
HITL Approvals: CVE-2012-1823 PHP Remote Code Execution pending human review
Tamper-proof audit logs: 50 entries · 8 critical · 6 agents · hash identifiers
TIVS Score: PI 1.0 · HA 6.0 · DE 3.0 · CA 1.0 — Gosmar & Dahl (2025) formula

PROJECT 02 · 2026

Analytiq

Live Demo → GitHub
StackPythonStreamlitGroq LLMllama-3.1-8bPlotlyPandas

Three independent analytical modules share a common data ingestion layer. Each module is accessible via Streamlit tabs and operates on the same uploaded dataset session state.

DATA INGESTION CSV / Excel Upload → Pandas DataFrame → Session State | | | Analytics Tab NL Chat Tab AI Vision Tab Auto Charts Groq llama-3.1-8b Image Upload KPIs + Trends NL → Pandas ops Multimodal LLM Plotly Visuals Conversational Q&A Chart Interpret

Analytiq is dataset-agnostic. Tested against several benchmark datasets during development.

DatasetSourceRowsPurpose
Titanic Passenger DataKaggle891Analytics tab — survival distributions, age/class breakdowns
Sales Performance DataSynthetic5,000+KPI validation — revenue trends, regional comparisons
World Happiness ReportUN / Kaggle156NL query testing — "which country has highest GDP score?"
Stock Price HistoryYahoo Finance10,000+Time-series trend analysis stress testing
User-uploaded ChartsUser-providedN/AAI Vision tab — bar, pie, line charts for interpretation testing
8B
LLM Parameters
<3s
NL Query Response
3
Analysis Modules
Any
Dataset Compatible

Groq's llama-3.1-8b achieves NL query responses in under 3 seconds. Auto-chart generation renders up to 10 Plotly charts instantly. AI Vision processes chart images in under 5 seconds. Tested with 100 to 100,000+ row datasets with no performance degradation.

Product
Building Analytiq: Making Data Analysis Accessible to Everyone
Most business users can't write SQL or Python. Analytiq bridges that gap — upload a spreadsheet and immediately get charts, KPIs, and answers to natural language questions about your data.
LLM Engineering
NL to Pandas: Prompt Engineering for Reliable Data Queries
Getting an LLM to reliably translate natural language into Pandas operations is harder than it sounds. This covers system prompts, schema injection, output validation, and handling hallucinations without crashing the app.
Computer Vision
Teaching AI to Read Charts: The Analytiq Vision Module
The AI Vision tab lets users upload any chart image and receive a natural language interpretation. This post explains the image preprocessing pipeline and real examples of the system interpreting complex visualisations.

Real screenshots from the live Analytiq deployment. All 3 tabs shown across 8 views below.

Analytiq landing page: upload CSV, Excel, or chart image — three tabs: Data Analysis, Chat with Data, AI Vision
177 rows × 11 columns loaded — data preview with filters for date, product, category, and region
Pie chart: Wireless Mouse 18.6% and Keyboard 17.2% lead; 14 products across Electronics and Furniture
Line chart showing upward sales trend throughout 2024, peaking in Dec with 300 units in a single day
Monthly aggregated bar chart: consistent growth from 487 in Jan to 1,765 in Dec 2024
Natural language query: "Give me a profit summary" — AI returns structured profit breakdown with Pandas code
LLM answer: Total Profit ≈ ₹621M · Electronics avg ₹352K · Furniture avg ₹344K · top 5 salespeople listed
AI Vision: upload any PNG/JPG chart or dashboard screenshot — AI will explain it in plain English

PROJECT 03 · 2025

Telco Customer Churn Analysis

GitHub →
StackPythonScikit-learnXGBoostStreamlitPandasSeabornJupyter

A classic end-to-end supervised ML pipeline. Raw telecom data flows through preprocessing and feature engineering, then into model training and evaluation. Best model served via Streamlit.

Raw Dataset → EDA + Cleaning → Feature Engineering IBM Telco Missing values Encoding / Scaling 7,043 rows Distributions Class weight handling | |———————————————| | | Model Training Streamlit Dashboard LR / RF / XGBoost Live Churn Predictor 5-fold CV Feature Importance Plot
AttributeDetail
Dataset NameIBM Telco Customer Churn
SourceIBM Sample Datasets / Kaggle
Total Rows7,043 customer records
Features21 columns — demographics, account info, services, charges
Target VariableChurn (Yes / No) — binary classification
Class Imbalance~26.5% churn rate — handled via class weighting
Key Featurestenure, MonthlyCharges, TotalCharges, Contract type, InternetService
PreprocessingMissing value imputation, label encoding, StandardScaler for numerics
82%
XGBoost Accuracy
79%
Random Forest
80%
Logistic Reg.
0.86
XGBoost AUC-ROC
74%
XGBoost Recall

XGBoost achieved the best performance: 82% accuracy and 0.86 AUC-ROC on the held-out test set. Recall was prioritised over precision given the cost of missing a churning customer. Top predictors: contract type, tenure, and monthly charges. 5-fold cross-validation used across all models.

Data Science
Why Do Telecom Customers Leave? An EDA Deep-Dive
Before building any model, I spent time understanding the data — surprising correlations, the dramatic impact of contract type on churn, and why MonthlyCharges alone tells only half the story.
Machine Learning
XGBoost vs Random Forest vs Logistic Regression: Churn Prediction Showdown
Three models, one dataset. Why XGBoost wins on AUC-ROC, where Logistic Regression is surprisingly competitive, and the key lesson about optimising for recall when false negatives are costly.
Product
From Jupyter Notebook to Business Dashboard: Deploying ML with Streamlit
A notebook is great for building a model — terrible for presenting to stakeholders. How I transformed churn analysis into an interactive Streamlit app with live prediction inputs and feature importance plots.

Screenshots from the Telco Churn analysis — Power BI dashboard, Streamlit app, and ML evaluation outputs. View the full project on GitHub.

Power BI dashboard: churned by contract (842 month-to-month), churn % by tenure, payment method breakdown, KPIs
7,043 customers · 1,869 churned · 26.6% churn rate · $139,131 revenue at risk per month
Churn drops sharply with tenure: 47.7% at 0–12m down to 6.6% at 61–72m — key business insight
